parameters.content:
## ♻️ Content Repurposer

**What it does:** gives you a private form URL. Paste one piece of content (blog post, newsletter, video script) and get back an email with four ready-to-post formats: a LinkedIn post, an X thread, an Instagram caption, and a short email blurb — all in the original's voice.

### Setup (≈5 minutes)
1. Open **Repurpose (AI)** → add your OpenAI API key.
2. Open **Email the formats** → connect your Gmail account.
3. Click **Execute workflow** once, then open the **Paste your content** node and copy its **Form URL** — bookmark it.
4. After publishing anything: open the URL, paste the content, submit. Four formats arrive in your inbox.

💡 Activate the workflow (toggle top-right) to make the form URL permanent.
💡 Agencies: duplicate per client and tweak the prompt with each client's voice rules.

— Free template from workflowstacks.com

parameters.formDescription: Paste one piece of content, get LinkedIn + X + Instagram + email versions back by email.

values.content:
=You repurpose long-form content into social formats. You keep the author's voice — you never flatten it into generic marketing speak.

Original content, titled "{{ $json['Content title'] }}":
{{ $json['Paste your content'] }}

Produce EXACTLY this structure in plain text:

=== LINKEDIN POST ===
120-200 words. Open with the single most surprising or useful idea (no "I'm excited to share"). Short paragraphs, one line of air between them. End with a genuine question to the reader. No hashtags.

=== X THREAD ===
5-7 numbered tweets. Tweet 1 is a hook that stands alone (under 200 characters, no "a thread 🧵"). Each following tweet delivers one complete idea. Final tweet summarizes the takeaway.

=== INSTAGRAM CAPTION ===
60-120 words. Conversational, line breaks for rhythm. End with 3-5 relevant hashtags on their own line.

=== EMAIL BLURB ===
40-70 words for a newsletter: what the piece is about and one reason to care. End with "Read the full piece:" on its own line (the author adds the link).

Rules: pull real ideas, numbers, and phrases from the original — never invent claims it doesn't make. Match the original's tone. No emojis unless the original uses them.
